content.full_text:
CITY OF FAIRFIELD
1000 WEBSTER STREET
FAIRFIELD, CA 94533
[707] 428-7419

Incorporated December 12, 1903

May 6, 1992

Fair Political Practices Commission
Conflict of Interest Division
428 J Street - Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

re: Waterman & Capitola Project: City of Fairfield

Dear Fair Political Practices Commission staff:

Please consider this letter a request for written advice submitted pursuant to Government Code
§83114. I am the City Attorney of the City of Fairfield and have been authorized by Vice-Mayor
Garry Ichikawa, Councilman George Pettygrove and Councilman Chuck Hammond to request
written advice from the Fair Political Practices Commission regarding their duties and
responsibilities under the Political Reform Act.

FACTS

Vice-Mayor Ichikawa and Councilmen Pettygrove and Hammond are members of the City
Council of the City of Fairfield. Bridge Housing Company has entered into an agreement with a
private party to purchase land within the City of Fairfield for the purpose of building housing
affordable to moderate income families. This letter will refer to that proposal as the Waterman &
Capitola Project as the site for the project is located at the corner of Waterman & Capitola
Boulevards in Fairfield. The Waterman & Capitola Project is proposed to be a for-sale townhome
project at a density of 12-14 units per acre. The site for the Project is vacant, the General Plan
designation is medium density residential (8 units/acre). The existing neighborhood within
one-half mile of the Project contains single-family residences constructed to an average density of
4 units per acre. Waterman Boulevard is an arterial street; Capitola Way is a collector street.

The personal residences of Vice-Mayor Ichikawa (3001 Rustle Ct.) and Councilmen Pettygrove
(1970 Barbour Dr.) and Hammond (2680 Mankas Blvd.) each are outside a 300 foot radius but
within a 2500 foot radius of the site of the Waterman & Capitola Project. On June 16, 1992, the
City Council, sitting as a Redevelopment Agency, will be asked to consider contributing $1.4
million in Agency funds towards the acquisition of the land. At a subsequent meeting, the City
Council, sitting as a City Council, will be asked to approve certain land use entitlements to allow
the Waterman & Capitola Project to proceed.

QUESTION

Are Vice-Mayor Ichikawa and Councilmen Pettygrove and Hammond prohibited from
participating in decisions about the Waterman & Capitola Project because their homes are outside
a 300 foot but within a 2500 foot radius of the Project site?

DISCUSSION

The City has looked at two separate studies to analyze any potential economic impacts to the three
councilmembers who live within one-half mile of a proposed affordable multi-family townhouse
ownership project. The studies look at impacts on existing, single family homes from
development of an affordable multi-family project. One study was not site specific and prepared
by California Department of Housing and Community Development. This study which is
attached, concluded that affordable and subsidized housing has not had a significant effect on
nearby market rate homes.

The second study was commissioned by the City and conducted by Sedway and Associates.
Sedway and Associates was asked to analyze the potential financial impacts on single-family
housing surrounding the Waterman and Capitola Project. Specifically, the study analyzed to
determine if an expected $10,000, positive or negative, change in value in these councilmembers'
homes could be anticipated by development of the Waterman and Capitola Project on the site.

Sedway concluded that the impact of a multi-family townhouse development on the site will be
minimal on sales values of existing homes within a one-half mile radius, and upon homes occupied
by councilmembers within this radius.

The methodology used by Sedway was to analyze three developments comparable to the one
proposed, within neighborhoods similar to those surrounding the site. Since each of the three
councilmembers live at a different distance from the site, the potential impacts were analyzed
using the comparable developments at different distances. The three comparable developments
are all in Marin County. The three are similar to the proposed project based upon number of units,
density, inclusion of below market rate units, and proximity to established single-family detached
homes.

Sedway analyzed sale prices of single-family detached homes both prior to and after development
of the multi-family units. The analysis was collected both on a sales price of the home and a sales
price per square foot to account for different size of houses. While the sample of homes sold may
not be statistically significant, based on standard statistical sample analysis, Sedway believes the
analysis provides a representative impact.

It should be noted that any impact to values around the comparable developments can be attributed
to a number of factors (i.e. economic conditions, number of available homes for sale at any one
time, condition of each home, etc). However, all other factors were assumed constant and for
purposes of the analysis, Sedway assumed that all impacts on values were fully attributable to the
multi-family development.

Sedway concluded that the impact on the single-family home values around the councilmember's
home at 2680 Mankas Boulevard, would be a positive 1.9% increase in single-family housing
value (3.3% on a per square foot basis). This is based upon pre-construction value of $106 per
square foot and post construction value of $110 per square foot.

For single-family home values around the councilmember's home at 3001 Rustle Court, Sedway
estimated the impact on home values to be an increase in home values from $101 per square foot
pre-construction value to $106 per square foot post construction value.

Finally, for single-family home values around the councilmember's house at 1972 Barbour Drive,
Sedway estimated the impact to be an increase in home values from the pre-construction average
sales price of $96 per square foot to $105 per square foot post-construction.

Sedway's findings are that a potential conflict of interest, based upon the $10,000 threshold, was at 1972 Barbour Drive using the cost per square foot analysis. In that case home values on a per square foot basis went up 8 percent, or $16,300. However, Sedway concluded that since this house is farthest from the proposed site, the finding is counter-intuitive and believe that the impact is due to statistical error or a result of other factors.

Applying the increase in per square foot value, pre-construction versus post construction, for 2680 Mantua Blvd. and to 3001 Rustle Court to the estimate square footage of these homes, there may, additionally, be an increase of value of greater than $10,000 at 3001 Rustle Court. The increase in value is $5 per square foot for a home in excess of 2,000 square feets.

In spite of these indications based on mathematical analysis of potential conflicts, the Sedway study finds that the impact of a multi-family townhouse development on the site will be minimal to existing home values within a one-half mile radius. They further conclude that the monetary impact, if any, on the three councilmembers will be less than $10,000, positive or negative. The Sedway study is attached for your information.

I understand that §18702.3(d) require an analysis of the following factors as well:

1. The proximity of the property which is the subject of the decision and the magnitude of the proposed project or change in use in relationship to the property in which the official has an interest;

2. Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect the development potential or income producing potential of the property;

3. Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will result in a change to the character of the neighborhood.

Part of the reason for this request for written advice, is to ask for your written advice on the appropriate manner to apply the factors contained in §18702.3(d) to this fact situation.

Thank you for your attention to this request. I would be happy to answer any questions you may have. The first City Council decision on this project will be June 16, 1992. Therefore, your advice in advance of that date would be appreciated.

Sincerely,

[illegible]

Betsy Strauss
City Attorney


c:     Mayor and City Council
       City Manager
       Economic Development Manager

a 300 foot but within a 25A0 foot radius of the Project site?
